project: Pulse Forge AI Release Copilot Homepage
product_context: A homepage for an AI release copilot that helps engineering teams summarize pull requests, generate release notes, flag risky deploys, and coordinate rollout checklists.
design_goals: Make the product feel trustworthy to engineers and technical decision-makers.
Show product truth quickly through believable release and deployment surfaces.
Balance calm confidence with enough visual personality to avoid generic devtool UI.
constraints: No brand-copying from Railway, PostHog, or VoidZero.
Prefer lightweight effects over heavy runtime rendering.
Keep the first page build achievable in a standard React or Next.js stack.
layout_rules: Lead with one dominant value frame and put product proof immediately in or below the hero.
Use a recognizable shell such as a release dashboard, deploy panel, or risk-insight canvas.
Alternate denser proof sections with calmer reset zones so the page does not feel like a wall of tooling.
typography_rules: Keep headlines short and technically legible.
Use one typographic move for personality, but keep the rest of the system disciplined.
Constrain line length in hero and benefit copy.
component_rules: Use one hero product shell, one selector-driven detail pane, one trust metrics strip, and one structured utility footer.
Show release notes, risk alerts, deploy checklists, or pull-request summaries as the main proof surfaces.
Keep CTA hierarchy simple: one primary start action and one secondary product-tour action.
motion_rules: Use motion to switch views, reveal state, and confirm interactions.
Favor tab changes, panel swaps, and subtle reveal motion over cinematic effects.
Provide reduced-motion-safe behavior for any animated counters or dashboards.
copy_rules: Use plain-language benefits before technical detail.
Keep CTA labels literal, such as Start free, View the product, or See a release demo.
Frame the product around clarity, confidence, and safer shipping rather than vague AI magic.
accessibility_rules: Preserve semantic structure in dashboard-like shells.
Check dark-surface contrast for metadata, status chips, and muted borders.
Ensure selectors and tablists are keyboard reachable and clearly labeled.
performance_rules: Optimize screenshots, hero artwork, and any animated panels first.
Treat advanced rendering as optional enhancement, not a requirement.
Keep the first build compatible with a lightweight static or hybrid deployment.
anti_patterns: Using PostHog-style faux desktop humor without matching audience fit.
Adding fake technical metrics that feel unbelievable.
Overloading the hero with too many dashboard states at once.
notes: Recommended stack: Next.js, React, Tailwind or equivalent utility-first CSS.
Recommended first build output: one landing page with hero, proof strip, selector-driven feature section, trust section, CTA band, and footer.
